item.title: Re: +150
item.content:encoded: A long-overdue benchmark. I'm very proud of the Chicago Spirit's renaissance in the year and a half since!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the pathogen sapient? Its adaptative capacity is overall. Also, will not be strange that the patoghen mimiced bottle of spring water

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Been reading through all of the Spirit's stuff in preparation for my own attempt, and I've got to say that I enjoyed this start to finish. It's tough to pull off "the Foundation was actually behind [major historical event]" but it's done quite believably here. +1
Nice! I'm glad to see this one finally hit the centennial. It's definitely my most underappreciated article. Expect more Chicago Spirit in the near future!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was left slightly disappointed by the lack of actual freezing gorillas in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The historical aspect is really cool, and the descriptions of the little girl consuming herself through her wounds is going to stick with me for awhile. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2. Math is descriptive, not prescriptive. We made up the idea of integers following our observations. Even if we discover an unknown integer, nothing breaks down, we just move everything to the right one step. It's like with the umbrella hotel, an extra man came in, so what? We have infinite umbrellas.

4. Actually, when you prove that an equation has a solution, but the solution is an integer higher than 3 and lower than 4, what happens is not that you break reality, but a contradiction that proves that no such integer exists.

Therein lies the problem. SCP-033 is a proof that there is, in fact, a number that satisfies the equation, and that all previous observations have been based on an invalid assumption. The formula is not the problem. The invalid framework based on flawed perception is. The effects of 033 are the result of reality reasserting itself properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Embarrassingly late answer but the title is a reference to the Bolivian tree lizard cleanup plan from The Simpsons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Judging by the presumably commonplace nature of soda in glass bottles in the US and the *nix-type command line, the latest revision would be from about the 70's-90's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. I enjoyed the documentation that was obviously from the past, really quite well done. But what does the title mean?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
huh, I presume the latest one is at the very earliest, the 1970s, given how it is accessed through the Foundation SCIPNET -> suggesting the use of computers. The second form was determine to be "erroneous as of 1933", which means that there the latest version was 1933-> "present", which should be dated to 1970s or later.

Secondly, I believe the foundation has at least a dedicated small team of researchers to every SCP, particularly when this is a Keter class SCP. The fact that this virus, an anomaly that is at least has some grounding in science, has yet to be analysed in 70 years is disconcerting, and meta breaking. It's a virus, not a magic insect, and they had 70++ years to study it. Computers has evolved faster than that.

I mean, this isn't very significant, and I upvoted anyway, but the only way I can imagine the Foundation being this inept in the research of a Keter class SCP involves far-fetched theories, such as, the virus being a genetically engineered organism from a technologically advanced alternate universe, etc.

This article is cleared for "Level 4", so thinking about it further, I cannot seem to find a reasonable excuse for such gaps in the documentation. It would be appropriate for Level 3 clearance though, that would solve most of my issues (related to incomplete writing), I think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To give the article the benefit of a doubt, the latest version does not state any years. Hence, its latest date is early 1900s. As such, the article could be dated in the past and the present can be much different.

Alternatively, it is also possible for the Foundation to decide not to study the virus when there are a lot of more pressing concerns to deal with. While the Foundation is advanced in technology, they are ultimately run by humans who cannot fully devote themselves to every single weird thing out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, this virus originated in the 1900s, and 100 years it's still not understood? What has the foundation been doing? It should have been EX by now! Its a virus that is being studied by the most advanced organization in the world, and even after 100 years with what should be a dedicated team working on it, it's still not understood.

This is a great skip though, so +1, but this conflicts a bit with my headcanon which makes the Foundation sound a bit inept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. The premise is just too completely ridiculous to buy. Let's say Ѳ is more than three and less than four. I have four sheep, and I eat one. What do I get?

2. Math is descriptive, not prescriptive. We made up the idea of integers following our observations. Even if we discover an unknown integer, nothing breaks down, we just move everything to the right one step. It's like with the umbrella hotel, an extra man came in, so what? We have infinite umbrellas.

3. We have more ridiculous concepts. Infamously, complex numbers were regarded as such complete balooney that Lewis Carroll is thought to have written Alice to make fun of a bullshit world where complex numbers are a serious thing that exists. Complex numbers break down exactly nothing.

4. Actually, when you prove that an equation has a solution, but the solution is an integer higher than 3 and lower than 4, what happens is not that you break reality, but a contradiction that proves that no such integer exists.

5. In conclusion, what is obviously going on is that Ѳ is something like a rune or a magic spell or an equivalent of an infohazard but for reality, i.e. the formula is the problem here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See this post I made explaining SCP-033. It doesn't break math. It fixes math.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll echo whoever above said that anomalies don't have to follow accepted science.

There's "not following accepted science" and then there's SCP-033 "an extra integer that causes maths to break down" which is equivalent to "an extra mammal that makes biology break down".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it doesn't have any of the taxonomic characteristics of Monera.

I misremembered, it's apparently a virus rather than a bacterium. Which doesn't alleviate my concerns

Probably not, mimicry isn't compatible with the successful equine adaptations and would be selected against.

Funny, that: I'll point you at zebras, whose black and white stripes seem to be mimicry, of the "can't tell one from the other" variety and until recently thought to be of "blend in with the plants' shadows or whatever" variety. surely zebras could blend in better. at risk of sounding like a sci fi cliche, insects evolve faster.

That said, yes this is way too fast. It is also an anomaly.

I didn't bring up the sky sloths to just insult you for no reason.

Sky sloths work because some guy did some artificial selection, based on anomalous traits, possibly using some wunderwaffle that lets him speed breeding up. And still these sloths are not literate.

Natural selection works off throwing shit at the wall and seeing what sticks. it's not sticky, but there's a HUGE amount of shit. if these things evolved in the wild, Earth should be just completely full of random bottles of random shapes and sizes before they just stumble onto accurate reproductions of coke bottles. I'm not buying the "oh it's anomalous". It copies coke bottles (and no other soft drink) and learned how to do that instantly.

It's cognizant of its surroundings or the process is being controlled by an entity that is.

There's a reason they mimic coke bottles. Brand awareness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is some sort of bacterium,

I don't have any idea why you think that is the case, it doesn't have any of the taxonomic characteristics of Monera.

The reason there are insects that look like sticks but no horses that look like sticks is because insects breed very very fast and there's very very many of them.

Probably not, mimicry isn't compatible with the successful equine adaptations and would be selected against. There are definitely vertebrate mimics!
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flatfish
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leafy_seadragon
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kingsnake

That said, yes this is way too fast. It is also an anomaly. Being supernaturally fast doesn't also mean that it is necessarily supernaturally smart.

You know, probably not. People will drink store-brand soda, but they will not drink obviously counterfeit shit.

You seem to think that everyone pays very close attention to the details in their environment at all times. I disagree, and I think that there are several studies of human perception that back this up. But this is one of those things that it is very hard to convince people of (perhaps because of the natural desire to believe that one's own perception is inviable), so whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mimicry is a real thing. Mimicry in macrofauna is also generally "blend in with the background" thing. The reason there are insects that look like sticks but no horses that look like sticks is because insects breed very very fast and there's very very many of them.

This is some sort of bacterium, but I'll classify this as a macrofauna because it's big as fuck and also spreads under very odd and very specific conditions.

People would probably still drink vaguely coke shaped bottles labeled "Cccc-ooo"

You know, probably not. People will drink store-brand soda, but they will not drink obviously counterfeit shit. Whiskey is an another question

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gah! Hipster time pirates. That is the worst.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not 100% on the theory yet, but I prefer to think the Foundation is being had.

First thing, about the virus's "accelerated evolution", I'll echo whoever above said that anomalies don't have to follow accepted science. If we consider this SCP as a motivational being (or product of one), and apply Occam's Razor to it's actions and effects, does it make more sense for this thing to be evolving for natural selection as a mimic, or does it just really, really want to be beer?

Given that we've already got one alcohol-peddling GoI and most of the action takes place during a large national moral and political upheaval over alcohol, I don't think it's unlikely that other actors are involved.

I sense three possible levels of shenanigans going on here:

1) This is a strain of smallpox that wants to be beverages, and the Foundation's response is unfortunately mutating it for the better.

2) Somebody created this in an attempt to anomalously produce alcohol, it went terribly wrong, and the Foundation's response etc. etc. etc.

3) Someone specifically designed this thing such that it not only spreads but can be contained, and that every step in it's containment will move it closer and closer to perfectly producing facsimile beverages. They might have engineered these weaknesses in anticipation of advancing medical technology, might have been too clever by half and designed in every countermeasure, or might be actively manipulating the Foundation. In any case, one day, the "virus" will be perfected and every human's vaccine's or municipal water or whatever have the exact right amount of base materials for them to start cranking out PBRs.

And then whatever alternate universe wants human PBR swoops in and makes a killing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh I have, but I have also seen sbemail #24 "The Bird".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ooh, you haven't read SCP-1784?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tag Team's probably been too busy to look at this one yet. I'm planning to tag it myself, but I need to read up on the tag guide first. Plus I'm not certain how the author tag works - or if it still works - and don't want to make Tag Team's job harder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How come this article does not have any tags?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mimicry is a real thing, with tons of examples in natural history, surely they aren't all just due to our overactive Broca's region, especially since other animals seem to also respond as expected.

The idea that they can just suddenly make Coca-cola bottles willy nilly, as opposed to even "Cccc-oooo", because evolution, is insane, because evolution is not magic.

Magic evolution is, however. If this wasn't anomolous it shouldn't be here. That doesn't mean that the virus itself is sapient.

Also, who's to say that it isn't an imperfect mimic? People would probably still drink vaguely coke shaped bottles labeled "Cccc-ooo". They certainly would drink vaguely whiskey colored fluid out of unlabeled bottles.

FUK U FUKRE

Wite back atcha, Stwongbad!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Occam's razor slices Heikeopsis japonica rather reliably. It's a pretty good idea on paper, but people didn't actually fish for these crabs for consumption for a lengthy period of time, and it's most likely just pattern recognition. The idea that they can just suddenly make Coca-cola bottles willy nilly, as opposed to even "Cccc-oooo", on its own, despite not having eyes, because evolution, is insane, because evolution is not magic.

FUK U FUKRE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Once you accept that viral structures this large could exist (and obviously it would require a method of replication even stranger than Pandoravirus), then they certainly could evolve mimicry in order to get hosts to consume them without needing any intelligence. Pandoravirus itself mimics bacteria in order to get the host protozoan to engulf it. Human action has caused significant biomimetic adaptations in the evolution of other organisms, for example the shells of Heikeopsis japonica. The accelerated pace of evolution here is anomalous, but the end result itself isn't, and doesn't require an intelligent designer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think living was the wrong word to choose. I think sentient or intelligent would have been better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Viruses aren't sometimes included as living things because they can't reproduce independently. They still are subject to biological evolution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's the questionable fact that viruses are not living beings yet this virus somehow knows certain specific things in order to spread itself like liquid containers and soda pop.

That's what makes it anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's the questionable fact that viruses are not living beings yet this virus somehow knows certain specific things in order to spread itself like liquid containers and soda pop. And not just any liquid container, but ones specifically for the drinks Coca-Cola and Fanta. It's more like a predatory living and sentient pathogen than something like Thomas Hoang, the zombie prion, or the clockwork virus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Currently, it is indeed a virus. Specifically, a retrovirus.
What makes you think viruses can't adapt and evolve themselves?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really loved the letter from the gang banger, kept the whole article compelling and interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, so the virus went from exploding alcohol bodies, to shitting out alcoholic beverages during prohibition, to puking out safe-looking soda bottles. You sure this is a virus or some living pathogen adapting to its environment?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shiiiiiiiit

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
VER 3.3.0

The "bottle" and "bottlecap" are composed of silica and anomalous forms of keratin

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As anomalies go, "beer pox" is kinda just okay, but the amount of depth and story around this is fantastic. I can't believe I never saw the Chicago Spirit before, it's only two years old, but the time period is presented perfectly.

My only criticism would be the slang in the interview — are they really going to write something like "tryna"? — and a bit of confusion about the final iteration. It seemed like you were setting up the idea that the Foundation's efforts to eradicate this disease were mutating it, since hydrargyrum mercury shows up at the end of part one and the description of part two. I was expecting silicates in part three, but they never appeared, so it was a little disconcerting. But even as is, totally worth an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The new format looks nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's just the Army training.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image of someone shitting out a bottle is far too ridiculous for me to be grossed out by it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is some high-grade body horror. Now I want to learn more about Chicago Spirit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wowwee that made me squirm.

I really like looking at how the language and content of this document has changed over the years, as the disease, available tech, and political atmosphere around the article changed. Granted this meant reading variations of the same article three times, but it didn't drag at least.

Additionally, there is a great use of the Chicago Spirit, and of Three Portlands here.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I decided to emulate 2317's formatting method to hopefully maintain the previous story aspects of the collapsibles while making reading easier and eliminating chronological disjunction.

Please let me know what you think!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much exactly what I thought! Only one problem: why deliver food to a macrovirus?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stupid Foundation! You made me look bad!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Moving things around so that the time periods are together will definitely emphasise the fact that the symptoms of the anomaly have changed over time. I'm not sure how it will affect other elements of your story, however.

Seeing as you're safely in the positives, I certainly wouldn't make any changes on the site version. Might be worth playing around with on the sandbox, though, to see what you like better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the sake of readability, I would suggest either something like SCP-2700 or SCP-2317 in terms of format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To assist readability, would you suggest that I move the appropriate description and containment procedures to be next to each other?

Alternatively, I could combine the containment procedures and descriptions for each time period underneath a single collapsible each.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A very morbid, but strangely compelling read.

I had a chuckle at Eustace Bagge, though.

Is this from before he retired to live in a little farm in the middle of Nowhere?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I try not to neutral-vote these days, but I'm neutral-voting this for now.

There is a lot in here to like - particularly the Chicago Spirit section, which managed to steer back from the edge of caricature and keep my interest level high. And the concept of anomalous liquor being linked to Prohibition is a good one.

But for me the structure is working against readability - with three time periods jumbled together between Containment Procedures and Description (quick query, should the 1919 / 1916 dates on the "show" parts of the collapsibles actually be the same date?), and each of them quite long. It was hard work to keep making the connections between the relevant procedures and description, particularly as the anomaly changes over time. I'm afraid I didn't pick up the explanation for those changes, if it's there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it bad that I found myself not-entirely-repulsed at this?

Other than that, the idea is quirky and feels like something out of a twisted children's fairy tale.
Take a +1 and have yourself a lovely beverage of your choice.

item.title: The lizards are a godsend!
item.content:encoded:
I started writing this more than two years ago. I don't remember exactly when - but I do recall working on this in June 2014. I shelved it for a couple years because it was even more bloated garbage than it is now. Then the bug hit me to do a long-form SCP, and I figured, why not resurrect this, but better?

Shoutout to PeppersGhost for reviving The Chicago Spirit, one of the coolest Groups of Interest around.

See more of my work on my author page!

+ Image credits
- Hide image credits

smallpox.jpg: Gloucester smallpox epidemic, 1896: Ethel Cromwell
This file is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license. This file comes from Wellcome Images, a website operated by Wellcome Trust, a global charitable foundation based in the United Kingdom. Refer to Wellcome blog post (archive).

+ Crit credits
- Hide crit credits

First off, I owe a major debt of gratitude towards Captain Cain, TwistedGears, thekillerax, and GottaGoFeast, who looked at the first incarnation of this two years ago and convinced me that it needed a massive overhaul.
I also owe AidenEldritch big time for frequently re-reading it and hyping it up for others to critique.

Many thanks to Conwell and kol aumer for helping me compress the containment procedures.
Many thanks to TroyL, weizhong, Kate McTiriss, rumetzen, and Lex1nat0r for helping me nail down the antiquated writing tone and find sources from which to emulate said tone.
I also wholeheartedly recommend [www.archive.org] as an immensely valuable resource for anyone seeking first-hand sources for antiquated SCP writing.
Many thanks to thedeadlymoose for convincing me to rewrite the US Government's involvement in the SCP.
Thanks to GreenWolf, Sudypt, ThatGuyRiverside, and everyone else I bugged into reading.
